nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; rich soft-bottom habitats in current-prone channels, and rocky or coralline habitats near algae, at depths of roughly 10–300 m

diet.notes: Ambush predator that has been reported to prefer live foods such as feeder shrimp, ghost shrimp, and small fish. Many specimens are reluctant to accept frozen fare, though some can be trained onto frozen silversides or shrimp using tongs once acclimated. Generally advised to feed to satiation only a couple of times per week given the species' slow metabolism. Mammalian feeders such as goldfish are best avoided due to long-term fatty-liver concerns.

goodWith: non-aggressive saltwater fish too large to be swallowed
avoidWith: surgeonfish that may pick at skin algae

summary: The Rhinopias Scorpionfish (Rhinopias frondosa) is a cryptic, weed-like ambush predator from the Indo-West Pacific. Venomous spines and a strict live-food preference make it best suited to advanced keepers in a 300+ L species-leaning system.
careNotes:
The Rhinopias Scorpionfish (Rhinopias frondosa), often sold as the Weedy or Frilly Rhinopias, is a slow-moving Scorpaenid ambush predator covered in leaf-like dermal appendages that disguise it among algae, sponges, and coral rubble. It is generally considered an advanced-level fish: highly venomous dorsal, anal, and pelvic spines mean handling with bare hands is strongly discouraged, and stings have been reported to require medical attention. Gloves, tongs, and a barrier in the tank are commonly advised when working inside the system.

Colour variants in the trade include red, yellow, brown, green, and golden "frilly" morphs — all are the same species. Rhinopias periodically shed an outer cuticle layer (often accompanied by cloudy eyes and visible peeling); this is normal and helps shed parasites and detritus rather than an illness, though excellent water quality is generally advised around shed cycles.

Reports on minimum tank size diverge widely: LiveAquaria lists 40 gal (151 L) with a note that 70 gal (265 L) or larger is preferable, Tropical Fish Hobbyist suggests 20–30 gal (76–114 L) on the grounds that the fish 'barely moves', ReefApp lists 100 L, and Saltcorner recommends 100 gal (380 L) for a full-grown adult. Because adult specimens reach 20–23 cm, are heavy waste producers, and any handling carries envenomation risk, the cautious side of this spread (around 300 L / 75 gal with a long footprint) has been adopted here.

Temperature recommendations varied: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), ReefApp ~26°C, FishBase wild data 17.5–27.2°C. A practical aquarium range of 22–26°C is used. pH and dKH are taken from LiveAquaria (8.1–8.4 and 8–12 respectively).

Temperament is best described as peaceful toward similarly-sized or larger tankmates but unambiguously predatory toward anything that fits in its mouth — small fish, ornamental shrimp, and small crustaceans should be avoided. Reef-safe 'with caution' applies: corals are generally ignored, but mobile invertebrates are at risk. The species is nocturnal/crepuscular and tends to feed best in dim lighting; some keepers report shy diurnal behavior that improves with mature rockwork and limited disturbance.

Breeding in captivity is rarely reported and is generally considered impractical for hobbyists.

Venom note: distinct from boxfish/soapfish ostracitoxin-style 'toxicToTank' chemistry — Rhinopias venom is injected via spines and does not poison tankmates in the water column, so toxicToTank is set to false.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Tank-size divergence was the flagged concern. Re-consulted FishBase (max 23 cm confirmed, no tank size given), Wikipedia (confirms 23 cm max, notes diet-transition difficulty and disease susceptibility from live feeders), LiveAquaria (40 gal listed but own text says 70 gal+ required, i.e. ~265 L), and ReefApp (100 L minimum). The low-end figures (76–151 L) reflect the fish's low activity but ignore its adult size (~23 cm), heavy bioload, and the envenomation-risk argument for a larger footprint that reduces how often the keeper must reach into the tank. The 300 L figure (nearest nice value above the Saltcorner 380 L / 100 gal recommendation divided down to a practical middle ground, and above LiveAquaria's own 265 L qualifier) is well-supported as a conservative minimum for adult specimens. No source contradicts it as excessive. minVolumeLiters=300 retained as researched.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has rarely if ever been documented in the aquarium hobby; generally considered impractical at home-aquarium scale.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page for Rhinopias frondosa. Provided: 9 in max, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, sg 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, 40 gal min (with note 70 gal+ preferable), carnivore, peaceful, moderate care, reef safe with caution.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked: max size 23.0 cm TL, wild temperature 17.5–27.2°C (mean 21°C), depth 10–297 m, Indo-West Pacific distribution (South Africa, southern Japan, Caroline Islands, eastern Australia), family Scorpaenidae, nocturnal predator, IUCN Least Concern. Designated secondary (Reef2Reef wiki) returned 403; FishBase substituted from source-urls.json secondaries.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 23 cm adult size, ~26°C, ~sg 1.026, 100 L minimum, peaceful (predatory to small life), 'delicate / advanced' care, reef safe with caution, periodic skin shedding to shed parasites.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine
additional.notes: Cross-checked: ~9 in max, 20–30 gal suggested (low end of spread, citing low activity), feed to satiation twice weekly, venomous handling warning (gloves/tongs), normal cuticle shedding behaviour, surgeonfish-may-pick-at-skin caution. Reef2Reef wiki (designated secondary) returned 403.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding rarely documented; not considered practical for hobbyists.
